content.text: When an enterprise buys an agentic AI solution not directly from a foundation model provider but from a value-added reseller (VAR) or system integrator (SI), the procurement risk landscape changes significantly. The vendor does not own the underlying technology; it assembles, customises, and delivers it. That intermediary position introduces risks that standard vendor due diligence templates do not capture well — hidden subcontracting chains, certification claims that trail the actual partnership tier, insurance gaps in the indemnification chain, and SLA pass-through failures that leave the enterprise without recourse when the upstream model API degrades. Partner and reseller vendor assessment is the structured process of evaluating VARs and SIs before committing to them as the delivery vehicle for an LLM-based system.
What It Is

Partner and reseller vendor assessment is a multi-gate evaluation framework applied to any third party that will deliver, integrate, or resell an LLM-based product on behalf of its upstream technology provider. The assessment covers five substantive dimensions:
financial stability
(can the partner sustain a multi-year engagement),
technical competency
(does the team hold and exercise real skills in the target platform),
reference quality
(do past customers confirm claims made in the sales process),
partnership tier verification
(does the claimed Gold/Premier/Authorised status reflect a current, verified relationship with the upstream provider), and
contractual risk allocation

(are insurance, indemnification, subcontracting controls, and SLA pass-throughs adequate). A numeric scoring model — weighted by the relative importance of each dimension — produces a decision: Approved, Conditional, or Rejected.
Why It Matters

The intermediary model is extremely common in enterprise LLM adoption. Most organisations buying an AI-powered CRM assistant, document analysis platform, or automated workflow tool are not buying directly from Anthropic, OpenAI, or Google — they are buying from a partner who has wrapped those APIs. The operational risk concentrates at that intermediary layer for several reasons.
Undisclosed subcontracting:
A reseller may subcontract the implementation to a second-tier SI with no direct relationship with the buyer, creating a delivery chain the buyer cannot directly manage or audit.
Partnership tier inflation:

Certification and tier levels are sometimes quoted from a prior period. A vendor may have held Gold status but failed renewal. Without direct verification with the upstream provider's partner portal, buyers cannot distinguish current from lapsed status.
Indemnification gaps:

If the LLM generates harmful output or a data breach occurs, the buyer's ability to recover damages depends on whether the reseller has adequate professional indemnity insurance and whether the contractual chain actually flows liability back through the SI to the model provider.
SLA pass-through failure:
An SI may promise 99.9% uptime to the enterprise while holding only a best-efforts commitment from the model API provider, leaving a coverage gap when the API experiences an outage.
Financial fragility:
Small SIs with thin margins may struggle to fund model API costs during high-usage months, or may exit the market abruptly, leaving implementations mid-delivery.
Benefits
Structured risk scoring replaces gut feel with a defensible, auditable record of why a vendor was selected or rejected.
Early identification of contractual gaps (missing indemnification pass-through, no subcontracting clause) before contract execution, when remediation is low-cost.
Reference interviews surface delivery quality information that credentials and certifications cannot — communication style, incident handling, and post-go-live support practices.
Partnership tier verification prevents procurement of services from a reseller whose discount or support entitlement level is less than represented.
Consistent scoring criteria across competing vendors enables fair, documented comparison and simplifies governance approval.
A conditional approval path allows promising but incomplete vendors to earn full approval through a defined remediation plan rather than being simply rejected.
Trade-offs
Assessment cycles add procurement lead time — typically two to four weeks for a full assessment including reference interviews. For urgent procurement this creates schedule pressure.
Reference interviews require cooperation from the vendor's reference customers, who may be difficult to reach or reluctant to share candid feedback.
Numeric scoring models create an illusion of precision. Weights are subjective, and a 75-point threshold is a convention rather than a mathematically derived safety cutoff.
Partnership tier verification requires direct engagement with the upstream provider's partner team or portal — a step that some providers make deliberately difficult for competitive reasons.
Maintaining per-vendor assessment records creates ongoing overhead as certifications lapse and financial data ages; assessments require periodic re-validation for long-term suppliers.
How It Works

The assessment proceeds through a sequence of gates. A vendor that fails a mandatory gate (e.g., cannot demonstrate current partnership tier) is removed from evaluation before consuming further resources on technical assessment or reference checks.
Vendor identification:
Build a shortlist of VARs and SIs from the upstream provider's partner directory, analyst recommendations, and RFI responses. Confirm each is willing to provide evidence for formal assessment.
Documentary review:

Collect audited financial statements (minimum 2 years), Dun & Bradstreet or Moody's credit report, current insurance certificates (errors & omissions, professional indemnity, cyber liability), subcontracting policy, and contractual SLA templates.
Technical competency evaluation:
Verify certifications directly with the issuing body, review a portfolio of comparable deployments, and optionally conduct a time-boxed proof of concept (POC) against a defined technical brief.
Reference interviews:

Conduct structured 30-minute phone or video interviews with at least three reference customers at comparable engagement scale. Use a standardised questionnaire to score technical delivery, communication quality, and on-time delivery.
Partnership tier verification:

Contact the upstream provider's partner team or check the vendor's entry in the official partner portal. Confirm the tier name, effective date, renewal date, and any programme-specific entitlements relevant to your use case.
Scoring and decision:

Apply the weighted scoring model (described below). Score ≥ 75 → Approved for contracting. Score 55–74 → Conditional: define a remediation plan with a 90-day checkpoint. Score < 55 → Rejected: document rationale and notify the vendor.
Architecture
The diagram below illustrates the end-to-end assessment pipeline, showing how each evaluation gate feeds the decision matrix and how the three possible outcomes map to subsequent procurement actions.

attrs.alt: Partner and reseller vendor assessment flow: Vendor Identification leads to Financial Stability Check, Technical Competency, Reference Check, and Partnership Tier Verification, all feeding a weighted Decision Matrix that routes to Approved, Conditional, or Rejected outcomes

content.text: The decision matrix consolidates seven scoring dimensions. Technical competency carries the highest weight (25%) because delivery capability is the primary value of using an SI. Financial stability is second (20%) because insolvency risk can strand an in-flight implementation. References (15%) and partnership tier (15%) are equal because both validate the claims made in the sales process from independent sources. Insurance (10%) and subcontracting policy (10%) address contractual risk allocation. SLA pass-through (5%) is the lowest weight because it is usually resolvable through contract negotiation, but its absence is still a red flag.
When to Use It
Any engagement where an SI or VAR will deliver an LLM-based solution rather than the model provider delivering directly.
When the contracted value exceeds a materiality threshold (typically $250K+ annually) and the engagement spans more than six months.
When the solution will handle sensitive or regulated data (PII, financial records, health information) and data-processing liability must be clearly assigned.
When the vendor claims a specific partnership tier or certification that affects pricing, support entitlement, or contractual liability terms.
During periodic re-assessment of an incumbent SI partner whose certification status, financial position, or personnel may have changed since the original onboarding.
Situations where multiple competing SIs have responded to an RFP and a fair, documented scoring basis is needed to justify the selection decision to internal governance.
How to Implement

The core of the implementation is a weighted scoring engine that accepts evidence items per criterion and produces a normalised aggregate score. Each criterion's evidence is rated on a 0–100 scale, multiplied by its weight, and summed. The class below encodes the standard criterion weights and the three-tier decision thresholds.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Dict, Tuple


@dataclass
class PartnerAssessmentScorer:
    """Weighted scoring engine for partner/reseller vendor assessment.

    Criteria weights must sum to 100.  Scores for each criterion are
    expressed on a 0-100 scale and are provided as part of the evidence
    dict passed to score_partner().
    """

    weights: Dict[str, int] = field(
        default_factory=lambda: {
            "financial_stability": 20,
            "technical_competency": 25,
            "references": 15,
            "partnership_tier": 15,
            "insurance": 10,
            "subcontract_policy": 10,
            "sla_passthrough": 5,
        }
    )

    # Decision thresholds (inclusive lower bound)
    approved_threshold: int = 75
    conditional_threshold: int = 55

    def score_partner(
# ... (truncated)
Examples

The reference check interview is distinct from documentary review. It converts qualitative human testimony into a scored dimension. The following example shows a structured interview dataclass and the function that conducts interviews with multiple contacts and aggregates their responses.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import List, Optional
import statistics


@dataclass
class ReferenceCheckInterview:
    """Structured record of one reference customer interview."""

    partner_id: str
    contact_name: str
    contact_company: str
    contact_role: str

    # Scored dimensions (0-100; interviewer assigns after call)
    technical_delivery: Optional[float] = None   # Did they build what was promised?
    communication: Optional[float] = None        # Responsive, clear, proactive?
    on_time_delivery: Optional[float] = None     # Milestones met, no surprise delays?
    post_go_live_support: Optional[float] = None # Support quality after launch?
    would_rehire: Optional[bool] = None          # Binary: would you use them again?

    # Freeform notes captured verbatim during call
    notable_positives: str = ""
    notable_concerns: str = ""

    def composite_score(self) -> float:
        """Return average of non-None numeric dimensions."""
        scores = [
            s for s in [
                self.technical_delivery,
# ... (truncated)

A third code pattern worth encoding is the subcontracting policy checker — a lightweight validation that confirms a proposed contract contains the minimum clauses required before passing the subcontracting policy dimension:

from typing import List

REQUIRED_SUBCONTRACT_CLAUSES = [
    "prior_written_consent",       # Buyer must approve new subcontractors
    "flow_down_obligations",       # SI's obligations flow down to subcontractors
    "audit_right_extension",       # Buyer retains audit rights over subcontractors
    "data_protection_passthrough", # DPA terms apply to all subcontractors
    "termination_on_breach",       # SI must terminate subcontractor on material breach
]


def check_subcontract_policy(present_clauses: List[str]) -> dict:
    """Validate that a contract contains required subcontracting clauses.

    Args:
        present_clauses: List of clause identifiers confirmed present
                         after legal review of the draft contract.

    Returns:
        Dict with 'score' (0-100), 'missing' list, and 'compliant' bool.
    """
    missing = [
        c for c in REQUIRED_SUBCONTRACT_CLAUSES if c not in present_clauses
    ]
    score = round((1 - len(missing) / len(REQUIRED_SUBCONTRACT_CLAUSES)) * 100)
    return {
        "score": score,
        "missing_clauses": missing,
        "compliant": len(missing) == 0,
    }
# ... (truncated)
Real-World Example
A regional bank procuring an AI-powered document analysis platform shortlisted three SIs, all of whom claimed to be Certified Gold partners of the platform vendor. Assessment proceeded as follows:
Partnership tier verification:

The platform vendor's partner portal confirmed that SI-A held active Gold status. SI-B had let its certification lapse four months earlier (now Silver). SI-C was still in the certification process and should not have claimed Gold at all.
Financial review:

SI-B's D&B report flagged a judgment lien from a prior client dispute worth $1.4M. SI-C's annual revenue was insufficient to cover a twelve-month delivery engagement without payment milestones — a cash-flow fragility risk.
Reference interviews:

SI-A's references were strongly positive on delivery quality but noted a tendency to subcontract development work to offshore teams without advance notice. One reference explicitly flagged a security incident caused by a subcontractor with inadequate data handling practices.
Outcome:

SI-A received Conditional approval contingent on a specific subcontracting clause requiring written consent and audit rights for any offshore data handling. SI-B was rejected (lapsed tier plus lien). SI-C was rejected (misrepresented tier). The bank added the subcontracting clause and proceeded with SI-A, completing onboarding two weeks after the initial assessment cycle.
Common Mistakes
Accepting self-reported partnership tier claims without verifying directly with the upstream provider. Logos on a website or a PDF certificate can be outdated or fabricated.

Treating certification as a proxy for delivery competency. A firm can hold Gold partner status while its certified team members have all left the business. Check that named certifications are tied to current employees.

Using reference contacts provided exclusively by the vendor. Cherry-picked references are systematically biased. Insist on direct referrals from the upstream provider's customer success team or from analyst-curated reference networks.
Neglecting insurance verification for AI-specific risks. Standard professional indemnity policies may exclude AI-generated outputs or LLM hallucination liability. Confirm coverage scope explicitly.

Confusing SLA commitment level with SLA pass-through. A vendor may offer 99.9% SLA in its contract but only hold a 99.0% SLA from the model API provider — the 0.9% gap is unhedged exposure. Review the upstream SLA document directly.

Completing the assessment once and never re-validating. Certifications expire, key staff turn over, and financial positions deteriorate. Build annual re-assessment checkpoints for strategic vendors into the vendor management programme.

Scoring all criteria independently without checking for knock-out conditions. A vendor with a perfect technical score but no professional indemnity insurance should not pass assessment regardless of aggregate score. Define and enforce mandatory floors on critical criteria.
Best Practices

Separate the scoring committee from the procurement sponsor. The person who selected a vendor for the shortlist should not be the sole scorer — introduce a second evaluator to reduce confirmation bias.

Verify partnership tier before investing in reference checks or technical assessment. Tier verification takes one to two days and is a low-cost gate that can immediately eliminate a misrepresenting vendor.
Request reference customers at comparable engagement scale. A reference from a $50K pilot project tells you little about delivery quality on a $1.5M transformation programme.
Store raw evidence alongside computed scores. If a vendor disputes a rejection, you need the documentary record, not just the final number.

Define knock-out criteria upfront. Missing professional indemnity insurance, an active legal judgment against the firm, or a lapsed certification on the primary platform are automatic disqualifiers regardless of aggregate score.

Negotiate SLA pass-through clauses in the master services agreement rather than leaving them to the SOW. Upstream API SLA terms change; a contract clause that flows through the current SLA automatically is more durable than hardcoding a specific uptime figure.

For Conditional approvals, define the remediation plan in writing before the engagement starts. A vague promise to 'improve documentation' is unenforceable; a specific deliverable due at the 90-day checkpoint is not.
Performance & Scalability

For organisations managing a large partner ecosystem (50+ active SIs), running full assessments on every vendor every year is operationally expensive. Several practices make the programme scalable without sacrificing rigour.
Tiered assessment depth:

Apply the full seven-criterion model to Tier 1 (strategic) vendors. Use a lightweight four-criterion fast-track (financial stability, partnership tier, insurance, references) for smaller tactical vendors under $100K annually.
Continuous monitoring for knock-out conditions:
Subscribe to D&B change alerts for all approved vendors. A new adverse credit event or judgment triggers an immediate out-of-cycle review rather than waiting for annual re-assessment.
Staggered re-assessment cycles:
Spread annual renewals across the calendar year to avoid assessment bottlenecks. Assign re-assessment months based on vendor onboarding date, not a uniform January cycle.
Cached evidence reuse:

Insurance certificates, audited financials, and certification documents have defined validity periods. A certificate issued two months ago does not need to be re-collected for re-assessment; only items past their validity threshold require refresh.
Tooling & Ecosystem
Dun & Bradstreet (D&B):
The standard source for third-party financial risk ratings, PAYDEX scores, and adverse event monitoring for commercial entities. The D&B Direct API enables programmatic monitoring.
Coupa Supplier Risk Management:
Workflow platform for formalising vendor assessments, storing evidence documents, and routing approval decisions through governance chains.
Ariba Supplier Lifecycle & Performance:
SAP's module for supplier qualification questionnaires, certificate management, and periodic re-qualification workflows.
Upstream partner portals:

Anthropic Partner Network, AWS Partner Central, Google Cloud Partner Directory, and Microsoft Partner Center all publish official tier and certification data. Direct portal lookup is the only authoritative verification method.
Gartner Peer Insights / G2 Buyer Reports:
Analyst-curated review platforms that can supplement vendor-supplied references with independently verified customer experiences.
ISACA CRISC / ISO 27001 certifications:
For SIs handling regulated data, security certifications validate baseline data handling competency and can reduce the depth of custom security assessment required.

Security & Reliability
Security and reliability considerations in partner assessment extend beyond the vendor's own security posture to the entire delivery chain the vendor introduces.
Data handling in the subcontract chain:

Each subcontractor that touches production data must be covered by the SI's data processing agreement. Gaps in the chain — even a single subcontractor not bound by the DPA — can invalidate GDPR processor accountability.
API key and credential management:
Confirm that the SI does not hold shared API keys across multiple customer environments. Key isolation is a basic security hygiene requirement that many smaller SIs fail.
Model API failover strategy:

Ask the SI how the solution behaves during upstream model API outages. A well-designed system degrades gracefully to a fallback model or queues requests; a poorly designed one returns 500 errors to end users.
Prompt injection and model misuse policy:
The SI should have a documented policy for handling prompt injection attempts and a process for updating system prompts or guardrails when new attack patterns emerge.
Incident response SLA alignment:

The SI's incident response obligations must align with your own regulatory reporting timelines. A 72-hour GDPR breach notification deadline is incompatible with an SI contract that allows 5 business days to escalate a breach.
Mental Model

Think of a value-added reseller the way a building inspector thinks about a general contractor. When you commission a building, you sign a contract with the general contractor — but the actual work is done by electricians, plumbers, and carpenters who are subcontractors. The building inspector does not care who signed the main contract; they check whether the wiring is up to code, whether the plumber is licensed, and whether the building is structurally sound. If the general contractor hired an unlicensed subcontractor who did shoddy wiring, the building owner is still exposed to the risk — and potentially liable if someone is hurt.

Partner vendor assessment is the building inspection step for AI delivery. You are not just trusting the name on the contract; you are verifying that the people who will actually build the system are qualified, that their work is covered by the right insurance, and that every link in the accountability chain is intact. A Gold partnership badge on a website is equivalent to a contractor saying they have a licence — the assessment step is the equivalent of actually calling the licensing board to check.
Key Takeaways

Partner and reseller assessment is a distinct discipline from standard vendor due diligence — it specifically addresses the intermediary risks introduced when a VAR or SI delivers an LLM solution rather than the model provider doing so directly.

Weighted scoring across seven criteria — financial stability (20%), technical competency (25%), references (15%), partnership tier (15%), insurance (10%), subcontracting policy (10%), and SLA pass-through (5%) — converts subjective evaluation into a defensible, auditable decision record.
Partnership tier verification must be done directly with the upstream provider's portal or partner team — self-reported tier claims are frequently outdated or inflated.

Reference interviews are the most information-dense step: structured interviews with three comparable-scale customers reveal delivery quality, communication norms, and subcontracting practices that no document can disclose.
Knock-out conditions — missing professional indemnity insurance, an active judgment lien, or a misrepresented partnership tier — should disqualify a vendor regardless of aggregate score.

A Conditional approval path is more useful than a binary pass/fail: it allows a promising but incomplete vendor to earn full approval through a defined, time-boxed remediation plan, preserving competition and reducing false negatives.

Assessments have a shelf life. Certifications expire, key staff leave, and financial positions change — strategic vendors should be re-assessed annually using a lightweight refresh approach that only re-collects evidence past its validity threshold.

The security surface of an SI engagement extends beyond the SI to every subcontractor in the delivery chain; data processing agreements, API key isolation, and incident response timelines must be verified across the full chain, not just at the top.
